metric_value: By Peter S. Kastner, special to SearchCIO.com; Aberdeen Group analyst leading hardware platforms, pervasive computing, and semiconductors research.

Too many businesses have been hanging on to Windows 98- and NT Workstation 4-generation desktop computers. IT managers know that these PCs are past their prime, but have met stiff resistance from CFOs and CEOs who would rather hoard the cash saved by endlessly postponing capital asset replacement.

More than 50,000,000 aging PCs sit on desks in corporate cubes and offices around the world. They were built and sold before the millennium -- in fact, many were bought circa 1998 because of anticipated Y2k problems with even older Microsoft DOS and Windows 3.1 PCs.

Software does wear out. PC software is programmed and supported by businesses that need to be efficient and profitable, just like their customers. Ford only guarantees spare parts for five years after a car is built. Guess what? Microsoft will only support an operating system for five years after it is introduced.
It's time to throw the words 'fiduciary responsibility' around the boardroom, and get a PC replacement cycle going soon.
It's ugly out there in Cube Land. Dusty monitors surrounded by sticky notes and keyboards that show distinct signs of harboring biological activity -- are still in operation, which is the problem.
Microsoft ended Windows 98 support in July 2006; Windows NT 4 Workstation support ended December 2004; corporations largely migrated to Windows XP by 2005-2006.
